features.summary: Costco reported strong Q1 fiscal 2022 results with net income of $1.324 billion ($2.98 per share) versus $1.166 billion ($2.52 per share) last year. Net sales increased 16.7% to $49.42 billion, with comparable sales up 15% reported and 9.8% excluding gas and FX impacts. Membership fee income grew 9.9% to $946 million, while renewal rates improved to 91.6% in US/Canada and 89.0% worldwide. The company opened 9 units in Q1 and plans 27 net new units for the full fiscal year.
features.management_outlook: Management expects continued inflationary pressures in the 4.5-5% range, with suppliers announcing additional increases for early 2022. The company plans approximately $4 billion in CapEx for fiscal 2022, up from $3.6 billion last year, driven by international expansion and e-commerce fulfillment investments. Supply chain challenges are expected to gradually improve, which should provide additional sales opportunities. Management remains confident in their ability to maintain competitive pricing while preserving margins through operational leverage and vendor relationships.
features.risks: Primary risks include ongoing supply chain disruptions affecting 79% of import containers (delayed average 51 days), continued inflationary pressures on labor and freight costs, and potential consumer spending slowdown as stimulus effects diminish. Port delays, container shortages, component shortages, and truck driver challenges continue to impact operations. The company faces margin pressure from wage increases and higher fulfillment costs, while some seasonal inventory may not arrive before Christmas.
features.key_drivers: Key growth drivers include strong membership growth (52.5 million households, up 800,000), increasing executive membership penetration (42% of members generating 70% of sales), and robust e-commerce growth (13.3% excluding FX). Costco Logistics now handles 70% of big-and-bulky deliveries with over 50% capacity utilization. Market share gains appear sticky, with strong performance across categories including electronics, appliances, and fresh foods, supported by improved in-stock positions relative to competitors.
